---
title: "Supply Chain Cost Intelligence"
subtitle: "SQL-driven supplier analytics on US federal procurement data"
author: "Alvin Alias"
date: today
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-location: left
    number-sections: true
    self-contained: true
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{python}
#| include: false
import sys; sys.path.insert(0, '..')
import duckdb
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import plotly.express as px
from pathlib import Path
from src.data_loader import DB_PATH

sns.set_theme(style='whitegrid')
conn = duckdb.connect(str(DB_PATH), read_only=True)
```

## Executive Summary

This report analyzes **[N] federal procurement contracts** from USAspending.gov (FY2023),
representing **$[X]B in government spend** across **[N] unique vendors** and **[N] NAICS categories**.

Using SQL window functions and K-means clustering, I identified four vendor performance tiers
and quantified **$[X]M in potential cost reduction** — assuming a 30% re-sourcing rate for
Underperforming vendors.

> **The business parallel:** I ran a structurally identical analysis at Daikin North America
> and identified $5M+ in procurement savings — $2M direct (supplier re-sourcing), $3M indirect
> (lead-time reduction → lower safety stock). This project shows that analysis as a
> *reproducible, scalable data system*.

**Key findings:**

- Top [X]% of vendors account for 80% of total spend (Pareto concentration)
- [X]% of awards exhibit significant price anomalies (|z-score| > 2)
- [X] vendors classified as Underperforming (high cost + slow delivery)
- Conservative savings opportunity: **$[X]M** (30% re-source of Underperforming segment)

---

## Data

**Source:** [USAspending.gov Award Data Archive](https://www.usaspending.gov/download_center/award_data_archive) —
FY2023 federal contracts, filtered to NAICS categories with ≥10 active vendors.

**Why this dataset:** Federal procurement data is public, real, at scale (millions of records),
and structurally identical to private-sector supplier intelligence — same cost / vendor / category
schema as a corporate BOM. Choosing real data over synthetic is itself a demonstration of
analytical competence.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-summary
#| tbl-cap: "Dataset summary"
from src.data_loader import get_awards_summary
get_awards_summary(conn)
```

---

## Spend Distribution and Vendor Concentration

### Pareto Analysis

<!-- Replace placeholder text with real results after notebook run -->

The spend distribution is highly right-skewed — consistent with federal procurement patterns
where a small number of large contractors dominate total spend. The Pareto analysis below
confirms the 80/20 rule holds: the top **[X]%** of vendors account for **80%** of total spend.

```{python}
#| label: fig-pareto
#| fig-cap: "Vendor spend concentration (Pareto)"
# TODO: render figures/01_pareto_concentration.png or regenerate inline
from IPython.display import Image
Image('../figures/01_pareto_concentration.png')
```

**Implication:** Optimization effort should concentrate on the top-spending vendors.
Improving procurement terms for the top 20% of vendors by spend has 4× the impact of
improving the bottom 80%.

---

## SQL Analysis

### Vendor Performance Ranking

The following query ranks vendors within their NAICS category by cost efficiency × lead time,
using CTEs and SQL window functions — the same patterns I use in the
[Retail Returns Intelligence](https://github.com/aalias01/retail-returns-intelligence) project
for customer RFM and top-decile ranking.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-vendor-perf
#| tbl-cap: "Top 20 vendors by composite efficiency score"
vendor_sql = open('../sql/01_vendor_performance.sql').read().split('-- ── Save')[0]
vendor_df = conn.execute(vendor_sql).df()
vendor_df.head(20)
```

### Price Anomaly Detection

Z-score analysis identifies awards priced abnormally relative to their NAICS category distribution.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-anomalies
#| tbl-cap: "Price anomalies: |z| > 2"
anomaly_sql = open('../sql/02_price_anomalies.sql').read().split('-- ── Output 2')[0]
anomalies = conn.execute(anomaly_sql).df()
award_count = conn.execute("SELECT COUNT(*) FROM federal_awards WHERE action_amount > 0").fetchone()[0]
print(f"Anomalous awards: {len(anomalies):,} ({len(anomalies)/award_count*100:.1f}% of awards)")
anomalies.head(15)
```

---

## Supplier Segmentation

### Clustering Methodology

K-means clustering groups vendors into performance tiers based on:
cost percentile · lead time percentile · award frequency · spend concentration.

```{python}
#| label: fig-elbow
#| fig-cap: "Elbow and silhouette analysis for optimal k"
from IPython.display import Image
Image('../figures/02_elbow_silhouette.png')
```

Optimal k = **[TBD]**, silhouette score **[TBD]**.

### Segment Profiles

```{python}
#| label: tbl-segments
#| tbl-cap: "Vendor segment profiles"
vendor_segmented = pd.read_parquet('../data/processed/vendor_segmented.parquet')
vendor_segmented.groupby('segment_name').agg(
    vendor_count=('recipient_name', 'nunique'),
    total_spend=('lifetime_spend', 'sum'),
    avg_award=('avg_award', 'mean'),
    avg_lead_time=('avg_lead_time_days', 'mean'),
).round(0)
```

### Cluster Visualization

```{python}
#| label: fig-clusters
#| fig-cap: "Supplier segments — PCA projection (bubble size = total spend)"
from IPython.display import HTML
HTML(open('../figures/02_cluster_scatter.html').read())
```

---

## Cost Reduction Opportunities

```{python}
#| label: tbl-opportunities
#| tbl-cap: "Top vendor substitution opportunities (conservative: 30% re-sourcing)"
opp_sql = open('../sql/03_cost_opportunities.sql').read().split('-- ── Opportunity 2')[0]
opps = conn.execute(opp_sql).df()
total = opps['conservative_savings_30pct'].sum()
print(f"Total conservative savings: ${total:,.0f}")
opps.head(20)
```

**Methodology:** Savings estimated as `(current_avg_award - cost_efficient_benchmark) × award_count × 0.30`.
The 30% re-sourcing rate accounts for sole-source constraints, contractual obligations, and
transition costs. This is the same conservative framing used in my Daikin BOM analysis.

---

## Limitations and Caveats

1. **Lead time proxy:** Lead time derived from `period_of_performance_end - action_date`,
   not actual delivery date. This may overestimate lead time for contracts with options.

2. **No quality signal:** Federal procurement data does not include defect rates or
   performance ratings. The clustering is cost × speed only.

3. **30% re-source assumption:** The savings estimate assumes 30% of Underperforming volume
   can be re-sourced — conservative but still an estimate. Actual recoverability depends on
   contract type (FFP vs. T&M), NAICS specialization, and competitive landscape.

4. **Scope:** Analysis covers federal contracts ≥ $1,000 in FY2023. Micro-purchases
   excluded. International procurement not covered.

---

## Conclusions

Supply chain cost intelligence is not a modeling problem — it is a **data organization and
SQL problem** with clustering on top to make the output actionable.

The SQL-first approach here deliberately mirrors how procurement analysts actually work:
aggregate by vendor and category, rank by KPIs, flag anomalies, then hand off to Python
for the segmentation that SQL can't do cleanly.

The output — named business segments with quantified dollar estimates, not cluster numbers —
is the format that a procurement VP can act on next Tuesday morning.

---

*Report generated with [Quarto](https://quarto.org). Source:
[github.com/aalias01/supply-chain-cost-intelligence](https://github.com/aalias01/supply-chain-cost-intelligence)*
